overview.body: You've built an amazing road trip game — but there's one huge problem: everything vanishes when you quit! Imagine playing for 20 minutes, reaching Houston, then accidentally closing the program. All that progress... gone. This week, you'll learn to save your game state to a file and load it back when you return. Welcome to file I/O — the skill that makes data persist!

mentorQuote.quote: Files are how programs remember things between runs. Think of it like writing notes to your future self. When you save, you're writing everything down. When you load, you're reading those notes back. Same data, different session!

objectives: A save_game() function that writes your game state to a file
A load_game() function that reads saved data back
A main menu: New Game / Load Saved Game / Quit
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 6
programRequirements: Your program MUST use open() with 'w' mode to write the save file
Your program MUST use open() with 'r' mode to read the save file
Your program MUST use try-except FileNotFoundError when loading
Your program MUST convert numbers to strings with str() before writing
Your program MUST use rstrip('\n') when reading lines

rows.task: Write 6 variables to a file, line by line
Read lines back, convert types, handle missing file
New Game / Load / Quit at startup
Add mid-game save option after each leg
Full save/load cycle, verify file contents

timeGuide.strategy: Open your save file in Notepad! After testing save_game(), open savegame.txt in a text editor. You should see 6 lines of human-readable data. If it looks weird, something's wrong with your write() calls.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you're adding — everything else is your 2.3 code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- DEFINE YOUR FUNCTIONS ----  [NEW]
save_game()   writes 6 values to savegame.txt
load_game()   reads 6 values back (or returns None)

# ---- MAIN MENU ----  [NEW]
Show menu: 1. New Game  2. Load Saved Game  3. Quit
│  If Quit → exit program
│  If Load → call load_game(), restore all 6 variables
│  If New  → get player name, set starting values

# ---- YOUR 2.3 GAME (unchanged structure) ----

GAME LOOP (outer loop — each pass is one leg):
│
│── Show destination menu, get validated choice
│── Destination Gate (coin flip)
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP (inner loop — each pass is one turn):
│   │── Roll D20, move miles, check for hazard
│   │── Repeat until you arrive
│
│── Update trip totals
│── Check outcome: beach / dead end / continue
│── At a non-beach city:
│      1. Continue journey
│      2. Save and quit → call save_game(), stop playing  [NEW]

# ---- AFTER THE LOOP ----
Print trip summary
"Thanks for playing!"

checkpoints.title: Build save_game()
checkpoints.subtitle: Create a function that writes all your game variables to a text file.

content.quote: Before you start coding, look at the big picture. You're adding three things to your 2.3 game: two functions that handle files, a menu at the start, and a save option during play. Your game loop from 2.3? It's still there, untouched. You're just building around it.

content.body: A function that takes 6 parameters (player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped) and writes each one to a file on its own line.

This is your first time making data persist — it survives after your program ends. The file is just text, so you can open it in Notepad to see exactly what you saved. Human-readable data = easy debugging!

references.description: You wrote outfile.write('John Locke\n') to save a name. Now you'll write player_name the same way — but don't forget + '\n' to put each value on its own line!

items: Copy your Lab 2.3 code to a new file called lab-3-1.py and update the docstring
Define save_game() with 6 parameters (see What You're Building)
Write the 4 numeric values — remember to convert with str()

rows: Forgetting '\n' after each write
Everything ends up on one line — can't read it back properly
Writing a number directly
TypeError! write() only accepts strings. Use str(total_miles)
Forgetting to close the file
Data might not save completely — always close()!
Using 'r' instead of 'w'
Can't write to a file opened for reading

content.body: Step 1: Add this test harness at the END of your file (after all function definitions):

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 2
save_game('TestPlayer', 'Houston', 350, 5, 3, 2)
print('Check savegame.txt in your folder!')

content.body: Step 2: Run the program. Open savegame.txt in Notepad. Verify these 6 lines:
Step 3: Verify each value is on its OWN line (6 lines total, not 1 long line).
Step 4: DELETE the test harness lines before moving to Checkpoint 2.

items: str() conversion for all numeric values

checkpoints.title: Build load_game()
checkpoints.subtitle: Create a function that reads the save file and returns all the game data — or handles a missing file gracefully.

content.body: A function that reads 6 lines from the save file, converts them to the right types, and returns them as a tuple. If the file doesn't exist, it returns None.

references.description: You used line.rstrip('\n') to remove the trailing newline. Do the same for each readline().
You wrapped file operations in try-except FileNotFoundError. Use the same pattern here!

content.quote: Here's my test plan for load_game(): First, delete any save file and verify it returns None. Then create a valid save file and verify all 6 values come back correct. Finally, corrupt the file (wrong number of lines) and see what happens. Test the happy path AND the edge cases!

items: Define load_game() with no parameters (it reads from SAVE_FILE)
Read the 4 numbers — wrap in int() to convert
Return all 6 values as a tuple: return (player_name, current_city, ...)

rows: Forgetting rstrip('\n')
Strings have invisible '\n' at end — causes weird comparisons
Forgetting int() on numbers
Everything from files is a string — math will fail
Reading lines in wrong order
You get player_name where you expect total_miles!
No try-except
Program crashes if save file doesn't exist

content.body: Step 1: Make sure savegame.txt still exists from Checkpoint 1. Add this test harness:
Step 2: Run the program. Verify this output:
Step 3: Delete savegame.txt, run again. Verify: No save file found
Step 4: DELETE the test harness lines before moving to Checkpoint 3.

items: Returns tuple of 6 values on success
Returns None if file doesn't exist

checkpoints.title: Add the Main Menu
checkpoints.subtitle: Give players a choice: start fresh, resume their saved game, or quit.

content.body: A startup menu that appears before the game begins. If the player chooses "Load," you call load_game() and restore all their variables. If no save exists, fall back to a new game.

items: In your main() function, add a menu BEFORE any game logic
Display the menu: 1. New Game, 2. Load Saved Game, 3. Quit
If choice is 3: print goodbye and return to exit
If choice is 2: call load_game() and store the result
If load returns None: print "No save found" and set menu_choice = 1
If load succeeds: unpack the tuple into your 6 variables
If choice is 1: do normal new game setup (get name, initialize variables)

rows: Unpacking tuple with wrong index
saved[0] is player_name, saved[2] is total_miles — order matters!
Checking saved == None
Works, but saved is None is the Pythonic way
Not falling back to new game
If load fails and you don't handle it, variables are undefined

content.body: Step 1: Delete savegame.txt if it exists. Run the program and choose option 2 (Load). Verify:

content.output: No saved game found. Starting new game.

content.body: Step 2: Play the game a bit, then manually call save_game() with test data (or wait for Checkpoint 4). Run again and choose option 2. Verify you see:

content.output:
Welcome back, TestPlayer!
Resuming from Houston...
Miles so far: 350

content.body: Step 3: Choose option 3 (Quit). Verify the program exits gracefully.

items: Option 2 loads saved game or falls back to new game
Option 1 starts new game as before

checkpoints.title: Add Save & Quit Option
checkpoints.subtitle: Let players save their progress mid-game and come back later.

content.body: After arriving at each city (except beach cities), offer the player a choice: continue the journey OR save and quit. This turns a one-session game into a multi-session experience!

items: Find the spot in your game loop where you check for beach arrival
In the else branch (not a beach city), add an OPTIONS menu
If choice is 2: call save_game() with all 6 current values
Set playing = False to exit the game loop

content.body: Step 1: Start a new game. Travel to any non-beach city (Houston or Austin).
Step 2: Choose "Save and quit." Verify you see:
Step 3: Open savegame.txt. Verify it shows YOUR player name and current city.
Step 4: Run the program again, choose "Load Saved Game." Verify you resume exactly where you left off!

rows: Saving wrong values order
Order matters! Must match what load_game() expects
Showing OPTIONS menu at beach cities
OPTIONS only appears in the ELSE (not beach) branch
Forgetting playing = False
Without this, game loop continues after save

items: OPTIONS menu appears after arriving at non-beach cities

beforeYouSubmit: Docstring complete? Name, ID, Section, Description updated
All DEBUG lines deleted? No test harnesses left behind
Main menu works? All 3 options function correctly
Full cycle works? Save → Quit → Load → Resume at correct spot
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: NOT YET: Lists (coming in Lab 3.2!), with statement (save for function library in 3.2), readlines(), Append mode ('a')

specific: str() and int() for type conversion
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Lists, with statement, readlines(), Append mode ('a')

finalChecklist.academicIntegrity: This assignment must be your own work. You may reference the textbook and BookEx examples. You may NOT copy code from others, use AI, or share your code.
warnings: CodeGrade will test your save/load functions by creating and reading savegame.txt. Make sure you use exactly that filename (via your SAVE_FILE constant) and write values in the exact order specified. The tests depend on line order!

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read BookEx 6.1.1–6.1.7 — the patterns for writing and reading are exactly what you need
Open your save file in Notepad — see exactly what got written
Print what you read — add DEBUG prints after each readline() to see what you're getting
Check line order — you must read in the SAME order you wrote
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 6 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example — your primary reference
